observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the lapsing schedule for all
of its properties and reconcile it with the recorded amounts in the books of accounts
to ensure the correctness of the reported balances of the property, plant and
equipment in the financial statements. Also, maintain the subsidiary records for
property, plant and equipment or the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Card
(PPELC) required in Section 114 of Chapter 7 of New Government Accounting
System Manual for Local Government Units.

We further recommended that the General Services Officer also maintain the
subsidiary records for property, plant and equipment or the property cards
prescribed in the accounting manual and reconcile the physical count report with the
accounting records and investigate and clear found discrepancies, if any.

Likewise, we recommended that the General Services Officer check thoroughly the
recorded properties under Special Education Fund and those donated items and
include in the RPCPPE to reconcile with the accounting records.

We recommended that the Municipality observe strictly the guidelines set forth in
Paragraph 29 of International Public Sector Accounting Standard (IPSAS) 1, of the
Handbook of International Public Sector Accounting Pronouncements, 2020 Edition,
Volume I for the fair presentation of its financial statements and for providing useful
information to the users for accountability purposes and decision making purposes.

We recommended that the Municipality always adhere to the provisions of Sections
4(3) and 4(8) of P.D. No. 1445 that trust fund be utilized for the specific purpose it was
intended and exercise sound fiscal administration in the local government operations
provided not contravening to existing laws and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipality observe strictly the provisions of the
Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 7160; Section 3.15 of Local Budget
Circular No. 122 dated January 31, 2020 of the Department of Budget and
Management (DBM); Section 3.17 of Local Budget Circular No. 131 dated January 8,
2021 of the Department of Budget and Management (DBM); Section 3.7 of Local
Budget Circular No. 134, dated January 20, 2021 of the Department of Budget and
Management (DBM); Section 6.4.2 of Memorandum Circular No. 2020-088 dated May
21, 2020 of the Department of the Interior and Local Government and Section 28,
Chapter 4, Book VI of E.O. No. 292, s. 1987:
a. The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) comply with the posting requirement in
the procurement of goods and infrastructures of the Municipality under the
competitive bidding;
b. The Municipal Accountant account funds transfers in accordance with the
guidelines provided by the granting agency particularly Local Budget Circulars
issued by the Department of Budget and Management (DBM) as well as
Memorandum Circular issued by the Department of the Interior and Local
Government; and
c. The Municipal Treasurer return to the Bureau of the Treasury the
undisbursed/unexpended fund transfer pursuant to Section 28, Chapter 4, Book
VI of E.O. No. 292, s. 1987.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other concerned municipal officials
ensure the full implementation of development projects as planned and optimal
utilization of the 20 percent Development Fund which is primarily intended to fund
projects that shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic
development and environmental management outcomes.

We further recommended that the development projects/activities contained in the
Municipality’s Annual Investment Program shall be the ones to be budgeted and
implemented pursuant to Sections 106 and 305(i) of RA No. 7160 and Article 410 of
Act Implementing Rules and Regulations.

We recommended that the Local School Board, adhere strictly to the provisions of
Section 272 of RA No. 7160; Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 of the Department of
Education (DepEd), Department of Budget and Management (DBM) and Department
of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) and the two addenda of the Joint
Circular in the appropriation of Special Education Fund (SEF) that the beneficiaries
of the special purpose fund are the elementary and secondary schools of the
District of the Municipality of Mataasnakahoy.

We further recommended that the copy of School Improvement Plan (SIP) submitted
for audit include the certification of acceptance and approval issued by the Schools
Division Superintendent to show that the document does contain strategic
prioritization policies in the allocation of SEF to the schools.

We recommended that the Municipality pursuant to Rule 1, Section 3(g) of the
Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA 10121, mainstream its risk reduction
and management plan in the Municipality’s local development plans particularly the
Comprehensive Land Use Plan (CLUP).

We further recommended that the Municipality strictly observe Section 5 of Joint
Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 of National Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Council, Department of Budget and Management and Department of the
Interior and Local Government in the utilization of Local Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Fund (LDRRMF) and Section 5.1 of the Commission on Audit (COA)
Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 to:
a. properly account transferred unexpended balances of Local Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) from the General Fund;
b. revert to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund the funds in the
Trust Fund – DRRM that are more than five years;
c. return to the National Treasury the funds transferred to the local
47
government unit which purpose has already been served; and
d. utilize the fund to the programs/projects/activities for disaster prevention
and mitigation, preparedness, response and rehabilitation and recovery as
identified in Section 5 of the Joint Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013
of National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council, Department
of Budget and Management and Department of the Interior and Local
Government .

We recommended that the Municipality fully implement the approved Municipal Solid
Waste Management Plan covering the period CY 2016 to 2026 in compliance with
declared policy of the State to adopt a systematic, comprehensive and ecological
solid waste management program and knowing that the LGUs are primarily
responsible for the implementation and enforcement of the provisions of the Ecological
Solid Waste Management Act.

We recommended that the Municipality stringently observe guidelines provided in
Sections C.1 (5) and C.3 (1) of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of the
Philippine Commission on Women (PCW), Department of the Interior and Local
Government (DILG), the Department of Budget and Management (DBM), and the
National Economic and Development Authority (NEDA), to effectively plan and
implement GAD PPAs of the LGU and that five percent (5%) GAD budget shall
endeavor to influence the remaining 95% of the LGU budget toward gender-
responsiveness.

We recommended the Municipality submit the necessary supporting documents of
those transactions were deficiencies are noted and detailed in the annex.

Also, we recommended that the Municipality ensure that succeeding disbursements
are completely documented specially to those pertaining to PPAs which post-
monitoring is essential to determine if the goals are attained by the Municipality.

We further recommended the Municipal Accountant ensure the full implementation of the
perpetual inventory method in recording and reporting of all procured supplies and
materials pursuant to the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the following:
 The General Services Officer conduct annually the physical count of all Property, Plant and Equipment and reconcile RPCPPE with the accounting records to fully identify the discrepancies noted. Complete all the fields required in the prescribed Reports on Physical Count and the necessary remarks. With regard to disposal, complete the necessary documents and perform the subsequent procedures in accordance with the Circular. Likewise, ensure that all PPEs are fully supported with all the PPE Cards by the GSO;
 The Accounting Department submit copies of PPE Ledger Cards and Lapsing Schedule of PPE to the audit team for CY 2021 onwards. Have those records reconciled with the GSO and document the results to be the bases of adjustments, if any. Likewise, ensure that the Subsidiary Ledgers/Lapsing Schedule of PPE tally with the balances in the Financial Statement. Lastly, review the breakdown of the items per PPE account and ensure that there is consistent application of accounting policies in recording PPEs. If there is reclassification needed, prepare adjusting journal entry and have it supported with the necessary documentation; and
 The Municipal Engineer assist the General Services Office and the Municipal Accounting Office in the conduct of the physical count and reconciliation of PPEs particularly to their Infrastructure Assets and Buildings and Other Structures accounts. Ensure that all PPEs in those groups of accounts are accounted and verified.
observations.managementAction:
In this regard, we express the following comments and resolution:
The General Service Section was already coordinating the said observation to the concerned offices though there are still some adjustments to be reconciled and discrepancies to be identified. We have also started using property cards for listed properties owned by the government which will also be used for proper disposal.
The Office of the Municipal Accounting Department will submit the PPE ledger cards and reports using GAM forms which tally with the Financial Statement starting this CY 2021. Continuous reconciliation with our GSO is still ongoing and will adjust accordingly in the books for the variances and inconsistency of classification noted in the Lapsing Schedule of Property, Plant and Equipment.
Municipal Engineering will extend their assistance to the GSO and Accounting Department in the conduct of the physical count and reconciliation of PPEs particularly to our Infrastructure Assets and Buildings and Other Structures accounts to ensure that all PPEs in those groups of accounts are accounted and verified.
observations.reason: The submitted RPCPPE still discrepancy with the accounting records. Incomplete information was observed on the submitted RPCPPE. Copies of Property Cards were not presented for verification. Documents substantiating the coordination made by the Municipality in the conduct of the physical count of infrastructure assets were not presented. Copies of PPE Ledger Cards were not submitted to the audit team. The submitted lapsing schedule of PPE are incomplete and some PPE accounts were shown with ₱0.00 balances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipality:
 Abide strictly with the provisions of PD 1445, particularly Section 4 (2) that government funds or property shall be spent or used solely for public purposes and the pertinent provision of COA Circular 85-55A. Public funds are the property of the people and must be used prudently at all times with a view to prevent dissipation and waste. The disbursement of public funds must be shown to be necessary or relevant to the fulfillment of the official duties and functions of the government officers and employees; and
 We further recommended that the Municipality submit explanation/justification that the procurement of 2,000 pieces of umbrella was for a public purpose with the main objective of promoting public morals, general welfare, ultimately necessary, relevant and intend to benefit the public using the taxpayers’ money.
observations.managementAction:
The procurement of 2,000 pieces of umbrella was for a public purpose with the main objective of promoting morals, general welfare, ultimately necessary, relevant and intend to benefit the public using the taxpayer’ money.
Joining hands for Mataasnakahoy’s Interest, signifies that hand in hand every taxpayer is part of the LGUs Program Projects Activities (PPAs).
 A Slogan encouraging every sectors and stakeholders in the stewardship of Local Governance.
 It is for the promotion of collaborative efforts between the LGU and Mkean’s, encouraging every individual to proactively participate in setting the directions and objectives of government thrust.
LGUs should ensure and support, among other things, promote health and safety, preserve the comfort and convenience of her inhabitants. On the other hand, the procurement of the abovementioned umbrella has the following purpose and benefits:
 Can be used in times of typhoon and other calamities;
 In this time of pandemic, provides health protection and safety; and
 Big umbrella can accommodate family members not just for individual use.
Rest assured, we will abide strictly with the provisions of PD 1445, particularly Section 4 (2) that government funds or property shall be spent or used solely for public purposes and the pertinent provisions of COA Circular 85-55A. Public funds are the property of the people and must be used prudently at all times with a view to prevent dissipation and waste. The disbursement of public funds must be shown to be necessary or relevant to the fulfillment of the official duties and functions of the government officers and employees.
observations.reason: Procurement contravened PD 1445 and COA Circular 85-55A; Municipality provided justification asserting public purpose and benefits, but expenditure remains questioned and not accepted as compliant.

observations.recommendation:
Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal
Mayor require the following:
 The General Services Officer conduct annually the physical count of all Property,
Plant and Equipment and reconcile RPCPPE with the accounting records to fully
identify the discrepancies noted. Complete all the fields required in the prescribed
Reports on Physical Count and the necessary remarks. With regard to disposal,
complete the necessary documents and perform the subsequent procedures in
accordance with the Circular. Likewise, ensure that all PPEs are fully supported
with all the PPE Cards by the GSO;
 The Accounting Department submit copies of PPE Ledger Cards and Lapsing
Schedule of PPE to the audit team for CY 2021 onwards. Have those records
reconciled with the GSO and document the results to be the bases of adjustments, if
any. Likewise, ensure that the Subsidiary Ledgers/Lapsing Schedule of PPE tally
with the balances in the Financial Statement. Lastly, review the breakdown of the
items per PPE account and ensure that there is consistent application of accounting
policies in recording PPEs. If there is reclassification needed, prepare adjusting
journal entry and have it supported with the necessary documentation; and
 The Municipal Engineer assist the General Services Office and the Municipal
Accounting Office in the conduct of the physical count and reconciliation of PPEs
particularly to their Infrastructure Assets and Buildings and Other Structures
accounts. Ensure that all PPEs in those groups of accounts are accounted and
verified.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the
following:
 The Inventory Committee headed by the General Services Office conduct the
physical count of its Local Road Network and prepare the RPCLRN. Make use of
the template provided in Annex C of COA Circular No. 2015-008 or Appendix 63 of
the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs and complete the necessary
information in every field. Submit the accomplished RPCLRN to the Accounting
Office for reconciliation in the books;
 The Municipal Engineer provide cost segregation of every road networks owned
by the Municipality per road component for the prior and succeeding years.
Submit the road networks duly segregated per road component to the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal GSO; and
 The Municipal Accountant provide the necessary Road Network Ledger Cards and
have it reconciled with the balance in the Financial Statements. As to the
identified Road Network projects, coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to
provide individual cost per road component to be the basis in the computation of
depreciation expenses.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal
Mayor require the Accounting Department ensure that all real properties owned by
the Municipality are fully supported with subsidiary ledgers and ledger cards.
We also recommended that the Municipal Assessor coordinate with the
Municipal Accountant to secure copies of those documents lacking on the
procurement of land and submit it to the audit team.

We recommended that the Municipality conduct the periodic physical count of
inventories pursuant to Section 194 of the Government Accounting Manual for Local
Government Units to vouch for the physical existence of the year-end balance of the
reported inventories. Also, prepare the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories
(RPCI) and submit the report not later than January 31 of each year.

We reiterated that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to
effect the cancellation of the stale checks and the Municipal Accountant to make
proper adjustments in the municipality’s books of accounts to fairly present its
financial statements as of December 31, 2020.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant consider some of the most
important qualitative characteristics of the financial reports of the public sector that
make them useful to the users namely: relevance, faithful representation,
understandability, timeliness, comparability and verifiability.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly comply with the prescribed
accounting method in the Government Accounting Manual for Local Government
Units for Trust Fund to show clearly and fairly in the financial statements of the
Municipality the incurred transactions under its Trust Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the municipal
records with the province’s books of accounts to establish the correct balances of the
reciprocal accounts and present fairly the balances of inter-agency receivables and
payables in the financial statements.
Particularly, the Municipal Accountant request from the Provincial Accountant
the copies of the JEVs showing all the transactions recorded in the books of accounts of the
Province that should be taken up in the Municipality’s books of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Bids and Awards
Committee Chairman, Vice-Chairman and its members to ensure that provisions of
Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB) Circular 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020
are fully complied with particularly the documentary requirements; price negotiation
and the online portal posting. Likewise, immediately submit to the audit team copies
of the lacking documents such as Mayor’s or Business Permit; Income Tax Returns
(Annual Income Tax Return of the preceding tax year) and original copy of Omnibus
Sworn Statement (OSS).
We further recommended that the Municipality efficiently and effectively
implement the social amelioration program like food assistance program as
prescribed in Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 series of 2020 of the Department of the
Interior and Local Government (DILG), Department of Budget and Management
(DBM), Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD), Department of Labor
and Employment (DOLE), Department of Trade and Industry (DTI), Department of
Agriculture (DA) and Department of Finance (DOF).

We recommended the Municipality to submit the necessary supporting
documents of those transactions where deficiencies are noted and detailed in the
Annex. Also, ensure that succeeding disbursements are completely documented
specially to those pertaining to PPAs which post-monitoring is essential to determine
if the goals are attained by the Municipality.
As to those inconsistencies noted, provide justification to identify the reasons
on those observations, summarized below, as follows:
 Discrepancies on the amount given as financial assistance
 Documentation relative to the distribution of food packs to frontliners
 Price variances on the procurement of PPEs, medicines and supplies for Covid-19
response

We recommended the Municipality provide justification letter for the
observations noted on the implementation of SAP – ESP to the audit team. Likewise,
ensure that the terms, rules and regulations on the succeeding social amelioration
programs are strictly followed and implemented by the Municipality to ensure timely
delivery of relief to the constituents.

We recommended that the Municipality:
 Abide strictly with the provisions of PD 1445, particularly Section 4 (2) that
government funds or property shall be spent or used solely for public
purposes and the pertinent provision of COA Circular 85-55A. Public funds
are the property of the people and must be used prudently at all times with a
view to prevent dissipation and waste. The disbursement of public funds must
be shown to be necessary or relevant to the fulfillment of the official duties
and functions of the government officers and employees; and
 We further recommended that the Municipality submit explanation/justification
that the procurement of 2,000 pieces of umbrella was for a public purpose with
the main objective of promoting public morals, general welfare, ultimately
necessary, relevant and intend to benefit the public using the taxpayers’ money.

We recommended that the Local School Board, adhere strictly to the
provisions of Section 272 of RA No. 7160; Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 of the
Department of Education (DepEd), Department of Budget and Management (DBM) and
Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) and the two addenda of the
Joint Circular in the appropriation of Special Education Fund (SEF) that the
beneficiaries of the special purpose fund are the elementary and secondary schools
of the District of the Municipality of Mataasnakahoy.
We further recommended that the Board ensure Special Education Fund (SEF)
will be utilized for the intended purpose or according to the identified priority areas
listed in the Annual Implementation Plan (AIP) culled from the DepEd-approved
School Improvement Plan (SIP).

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the efficient and effective
utilization of the 20% Development Fund which is primarily intended to fund projects
that shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and
environmental outcomes. Any delay in the completion of development projects being
implemented by the Municipality definitely slow down the community development or
realization of development objectives intended for the constituents.
We also recommended that any changes/reversions/realignments shall be made
pursuant to Articles 417 and 418 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA
7160.

Based on the forgoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal
Mayor require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office
(MDRRMO) implement the programs and projects of the Municipality under 70%
Mitigation Fund within the intended time to avoid delay in the delivery of services
and benefits to the constituents. Also, see to it that in-kind donations are accounted
and reported using the required templates and forms as prescribed in the circular.

We recommended that the Municipality consider the guidelines provided in
Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of Philippine Commission on Women,
Department of the Interior and Local Government, Department of Budget and
Management and National Economic and Development Authority in the choice of
gender issues and/or mandates it intends to address in the pursuit of gender and
development.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly abide by the rules and
regulations provided in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 in the grant
and utilization of cash advances.
We further recommended that the Municipality refrain from using
reimbursement in the procurement of goods and services and strictly comply with the
mandated provisions of the Government Procurement Act to avoid suspension or
disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly follow the guidelines provided
in Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2019-01 dated May 17, 2019 to ensure
amended rates of fees or charges are reasonable and just sufficient to recover the
direct fixed cost and variable costs of rendering services and also create a local
government unit with business-friendly environment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the following:
 The Inventory Committee headed by the General Services Office conduct the physical count of its Local Road Network and prepare the RPCLRN. Make use of the template provided in Annex C of COA Circular No. 2015-008 or Appendix 63 of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs and complete the necessary information in every field. Submit the accomplished RPCLRN to the Accounting Office for reconciliation in the books;
 The Municipal Engineer provide cost segregation of every road networks owned by the Municipality per road component for the prior and succeeding years. Submit the road networks duly segregated per road component to the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal GSO; and
 The Municipal Accountant provide the necessary Road Network Ledger Cards and have it reconciled with the balance in the Financial Statements. As to the identified Road Network projects, coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to provide individual cost per road component to be the basis in the computation of depreciation expenses.
observations.managementAction:
The inventory committee of this Municipality was already conducting physical count on some aspect but has not yet started the physical count on Local Road Network. We already coordinated the said conduct of physical count to concerned office. By this year, we will conduct an actual physical count on Local Road Network.
The Municipal Engineer is currently working to provide and submit the cost segregation of every road networks owned by the Municipality per road component for the prior and succeeding years with General Service Section and Municipal Accountant.
The Municipal Accountant will provide the necessary Road Network Ledger Cards and have it reconciled with the balance in the Financial Statements. Likewise, we will coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to provide individual cost per road component to be the basis in the computation of depreciation expenses of the said account.
observations.reason: Physical count on Local Road Network has not yet started; segregation of road component cost is still being prepared by Municipal Engineer; Road Network Ledger Cards not yet provided and reconciled with Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Bids and Awards Committee Chairman, Vice-Chairman and its members to ensure that provisions of Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB) Circular 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020 are fully complied with particularly the documentary requirements; price negotiation and the online portal posting. Likewise, immediately submit to the audit team copies of the lacking documents such as Mayor’s or Business Permit; Income Tax Returns (Annual Income Tax Return of the preceding tax year) and original copy of Omnibus Sworn Statement (OSS).
We further recommended that the Municipality efficiently and effectively implement the social amelioration program like food assistance program as prescribed in Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 series of 2020 of the Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG), Department of Budget and Management (DBM), Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD), Department of Labor and Employment (DOLE), Department of Trade and Industry (DTI), Department of Agriculture (DA) and Department of Finance (DOF).
observations.reason: Procurements were posted and BAC conducted price quotations; however, documentary requirements (Mayor’s/Business Permit, ITR, OSS) were not attached for some suppliers and to be submitted; clarification on beneficiary targeting and compliance with Joint Memorandum Circular to be strengthened.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipality to submit the necessary supporting documents of those transactions where deficiencies are noted and detailed in the Annex. Also, ensure that succeeding disbursements are completely documented specially to those pertaining to PPAs which post-monitoring is essential to determine if the goals are attained by the Municipality.
As to those inconsistencies noted, provide justification to identify the reasons on those observations, summarized below, as follows:
 Discrepancies on the amount given as financial assistance
 Documentation relative to the distribution of food packs to front liners
 Price variances on the procurement of PPEs, medicines and supplies for Covid-19 response
observations.managementAction:
Accomplishment reports were attached to Hazard Pay Voucher for regular employee and Job Orders to substantiate the contributions made of the said activity in the measures of controlling the widespread of Covid-19. Attached herein are the lacking documents stated on the abovementioned observations.
The Municipality had exhausted its effort to canvass the abovementioned PPEs, medicines, and supplies for Covid-19 responses that were most beneficial to the municipality. However, in spite of these efforts, there were price variances in the procurement of PPEs, medicines, and supplies for Covid-19 due to the following reasons:
1. There was a surge of demand for PPEs, medicines, and supplies because of panic buying due to unprecedented circumstances brought about by Covid-19 pandemic. Because of the Covid-19 outbreak, the value of the abovementioned medical supplies immensely increased. It has been observed that surgical/facemask increased sixfold, medicines trebled, and PPEs doubled; and
2. Due to Enhanced Community Quarantine, supply availability had been hampered by a number of issues such as travel restrictions and lockdowns that have forced suppliers to shut down. However, the Municipality had to act quickly to ensure the safety of frontliners because they were the most valuable resource to combat the highly contagious disease. Moreover, we firmly believe that we cannot stop Covid-19 without protecting health worker first.
The Municipality will abide to submit the necessary supporting documents of those transactions where deficiencies were noted and detailed in the Annex. In addition, we will ensure that the succeeding disbursements will be completely documented specially to those pertaining to PPAs which post-monitoring is essential to determine if the goals are attained by the Municipality.
With the abovementioned observations, rest assured that the office would act upon your objective insight for the betterment of the delivery of basic services.
The Municipality had paid ₱97,500.00 as financial assistance to the families with members who had been infected with Covid-19. The financial assistance given to them was in the form of medical assistance, food subsidy and burial assistance. However, interview with the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer disclosed that the Municipality had not prepared/issued separate guidelines which could show the terms, conditions and the requirements for each beneficiary to be able to qualify for such assistance from the Municipality. Instead, the Municipality had been using the DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 11 series of 2019 as guide in giving those financial assistance. Upon checking to the cited memorandum circular, it was found out that this was the revised guidelines on the implementation of the assistance to individuals in crisis situation. From there, the Municipality adopted the amount indicated depending on the purpose/need of the constituent, to wit:
Item Amount
Medical Assistance P 5,000.00
Burial Assistance 5,000.00
Food Subsidy 2,000.00
Based on the post-audit, it was found out that we have provided a Burial Assistance worth ₱10,000.00 to a Covid-19 beneficiary. We have attached the copy of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 Series of 2020 as special guidelines of the provision of Social Amelioration measures of DSWD. The burial assistance benefit can reach the maximum amount of ₱25,000.00 and P5,000.00 for food assistance for Covid-19 related cases stated in Section 7.2.1 of the JMC No. 1.
The main goal of MSWDO is to provide quality service and assistance in every vulnerable citizen of the municipality. We truly appreciate your feedback on the inconsistencies and discrepancies on the amount of the benefit, hence, we attached the copy of the circular.
Your recommendations are fully noted and will served as our guide in implementing government programs prioritizing the financial need of the people.
Thank you very much and more power.
observations.reason: Supporting documents and justifications to be submitted; some payment procedures and beneficiary guideline issues noted (lack of overtime work program, absence of formal guidelines for Covid-19 assistance, missing distribution reports, price variances) and Municipality committed to submit missing documents and explanations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipality provide justification letter for the observations noted on the implementation of SAP – ESP to the audit team. Likewise, ensure that the terms, rules and regulations on the succeeding social amelioration programs are strictly followed and implemented by the Municipality to ensure timely delivery of relief to the constituents.
observations.managementAction:
In response to the above-mentioned observation for item no. 1, we are submitting the certification from 16 barangays stating that the 3,534 beneficiaries are in dire need and eligible for financial assistance in accordance with the DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 4.
With regard to item no. 2, we have attached the summary and the photocopy of the receipts issued to the 23 beneficiaries who have refunded the amount of ₱6,500.00 because of various reasons such as SBWS duplication, ineligible - living with government official and husband received the same benefit from another barangay.
As per Memorandum of Agreement, the LGU must ensure the delivery of assistance to the beneficiaries within seven days upon receipt of the funds from DSWD. However, the distribution happened on April 14-16 ranging from 7 to 9 days. We have encountered some delays in the preparation of the payroll because of some technical difficulties.
Our Office truly appreciates your effort in delivering your observations and recommendations on the implementation of the program. Nevertheless, we are humbly requesting for your understanding on the shortcomings that our Office committed during the implementation and rest assured that more quality of service in all aspect will be observed in the coming programs.
observations.reason: Certifications from barangays provided for beneficiaries; 23 beneficiaries refunded amounts and receipts provided; distribution experienced delays (7-9 days) due to payroll preparation technical difficulties; some documentary gaps remain (missing SAC forms, justifications) and are being addressed.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local School Board, adhere strictly to the provisions of Section 272 of RA No. 7160; Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 of the Department of Education (DepEd), Department of Budget and Management (DBM) and Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) and the two addenda of the Joint Circular in the appropriation of Special Education Fund (SEF) that the beneficiaries of the special purpose fund are the elementary and secondary schools of the District of the Municipality of Mataasnakahoy.
We further recommended that the Board ensure Special Education Fund (SEF) will be utilized for the intended purpose or according to the identified priority areas listed in the Annual Implementation Plan (AIP) culled from the DepEd-approved School Improvement Plan (SIP).
observations.managementAction:
In this regard, we express the following comments:
The items included in the appropriation of the Office of the District Supervisor in the Mataasnakahoy District namely: Electricity Expense and Telephone Expense will no longer be included in the SEF Budget preparation. The Office Supplies amounting to ₱186,557.00 and Office Equipment amounting to ₱450,000.00 were allocated to the different schools. Also in the SEF Budget preparation, the Other General Service will be allocated to the Elementary and Secondary Schools for the processing of financial documents.
The Special Education (SPED) is separate from the District Office.
Likewise, the Purchase of Land amounting to ₱990,718.28 will be incorporated in the School Improvement Plan (SIP) of Calingatan Elementary School. Attached is the copy of the revised SIP of the said school.
Moreover, we will adhere strictly to the provisions of Section 272 of RA No. 7160; Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 of the Department of Education (DepEd), Department of Budget and Management (DBM) and Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG) and the two addenda of the Joint Circular in the appropriation of Special Education Fund (SEF) that the beneficiaries of the special purpose fund are the elementary and secondary schools of the District of the Municipality of Mataasnakahoy.
And we will ensure Special Education Fund (SEF) will be utilized for the intended purpose or according to the identified priority areas listed in the Annual Implementation Plan (AIP) culled from the DepEd-approved School Improvement Plan (SIP).
Our appreciation for the support and concern in bringing this issue for the improvement of the District.
observations.reason: District office items to be removed from SEF; office supplies and equipment reallocated to schools; purchase of land to be incorporated in revised SIP; commitments to strictly adhere to SEF guidelines made; some adjustments pending.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the efficient and effective utilization of the 20% Development Fund which is primarily intended to fund projects that shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes. Any delay in the completion of development projects being implemented by the Municipality definitely slow down the community development or realization of development objectives intended for the constituents.
We also recommended that any changes/reversions/realignments shall be made pursuant to Articles 417 and 418 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 7160.
observations.managementAction:
In this light, we express the following comments:
The Municipality of Mataasnakahoy thru the Office of the Municipal Engineer and the MPDC ensure the fast, efficient, and effective implementation and utilization of the project under the Twenty Percent Development Fund.
Moreover, we would like to inform your office that the project listed under the Annex C – Schedule of Twenty Percent Development Fund on-going project were all completed last February and April 2021.
Also, we are now observing the provision pursuant to Article 417 and 418 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 7160. Reversion of continuing appropriations is now part of Annual Budget preparation.
observations.reason: Some projects completed by Feb/Apr 2021; implementation and utilization being expedited; reversion procedures being observed in current budget preparations; earlier realignments/reversions had contravened Articles 417 and 418 but corrective measures underway.

observations.recommendation: Based on the forgoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRMO) implement the programs and projects of the Municipality under 70% Mitigation Fund within the intended time to avoid delay in the delivery of services and benefits to the constituents. Also, see to it that in-kind donations are accounted and reported using the required templates and forms as prescribed in the circular.
observations.managementAction:
In line with your letter re: Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM N0. 2021-017(20) dated May 14, 2020, which was received by the office, this 21st day of May 2021, the undersigned is humbly stating the interventions made to cope with the recent disasters, with deficiencies based on your observations and recommendations, to wit:
Despite of the long and gruelling disaster preparedness and response due to the recent Taal Volcano eruption and Covid-19 pandemic that brought to all stakeholders and actors an invaluable lesson to learn, the Office of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management is already exhausting its effort to enforce the implementation of the Projects, Programs and Activities of the Municipality under 70% Mitigation Fund, Continuing Appropriation and Trust Liability in order to strengthen the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management System.
In addition, this is the status of the implementation of certain PPAs mentioned on your AOM, to wit:
Purchase of Emergency Response Vehicle – implemented February 2021
Construction of Emergency Operations Center – on-going implementation, target date of completion June 4, 2021
Purchase of Modular Tent - for implementation this CY 2021;
Purchase and Installation of CCTV - for implementation CY 2021
Purchase of Medical Tent - reverted to 5% LDRRMF CY 2021
Moreover, the office of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office will coordinate with the General Service Office and Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office for the proper accounting and reporting of in-kind donations of the Municipality.
Also, The Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRMO) will effectively and efficiently implement the programs and projects of the Municipality under 70% Mitigation Fund within the intended time to avoid delay in the delivery of services and benefits to the constituents.
We would act upon your objective insight for the betterment of the delivery of basic services.
observations.reason: Some PPAs implemented (emergency vehicle), others ongoing or scheduled for CY2021 (emergency operations center, modular tent, CCTV); accounting/reporting mechanisms for in-kind donations to be coordinated and implemented; several projects remain unutilized or replanned.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality consider the guidelines provided in Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of Philippine Commission on Women, Department of the Interior and Local Government, Department of Budget and Management and National Economic and Development Authority in the choice of gender issues and/or mandates it intends to address in the pursuit of gender and development.
observations.managementAction:
Gender issue/mandate in the GAD Plan and Budget could be client or organization focused. Client-focused gender issue refers to concerns arising from the unequal status of women and men stakeholders, clients and constituents like lack of access of marginalized and indigent women to social protection programs and services of the LGU. MSWDO will improve its capacity to integrate a gender dimension in our programs, systems and structure as part of the organization-focused gender issue in the GAD Plan and Budget.
We are consistently including all sectors/groups of our society in the GAD Plan and Budget. These include women, men, solo parent, PWD, LGBT, and senior citizen. However, we failed to focus and explore on the equality of men and women as per Joint Memorandum No. 2013-01 but included and was specific on the discrimination against LGBT instead.
Your feedback is highly appreciated and rest assured that we will consider the guidelines stated in the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 to identify gender issues/mandates that will address gender equality and development.
Thank you very much and more power.
observations.reason: GAD Plan includes various sectors including LGBT though not aligned strictly with Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01; Municipality committed to consider JMC guidelines in future GAD planning.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipality strictly abide by the rules and regulations provided in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 in the grant and utilization of cash advances.
We further recommended that the Municipality refrain from using reimbursement in the procurement of goods and services and strictly comply with the mandated provisions of the Government Procurement Act to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit.
observations.managementAction:
Because of the Taal Volcano eruption last January 12, 2020 that resulted in the forced evacuation of residents in most of Barangays in our town to the evacuation centers and upon seeing the voluminous number of evacuees who were in dire need of food, medical supplies, personal necessities, shelter and others, had prompted the undersigned in the utilization of 2 million cash advances out of 5 million pesos Socio Civic Project Fund from Office of the President to expedite the necessary assistance in alleviating the hardship of affected persons.
Having said that, knowing that cash advances in spite of the occurrence of Taal Volcano eruption was not a valid excuse, we are conveying a profuse gratitude for the consideration accorded to us.
With the recommendation, we will strictly abide by the rules and regulations provided in COA Circular No. 97-002 in the grant and utilization of cash advances in the future.
observations.reason: Municipality acknowledges deviation from COA Circular and IRR of RA 9184 due to emergency response; commits to strictly adhere to COA Circular No. 97-002 and procurement rules in future; some reimbursements and cash advances already liquidated but procedural noncompliance remains an issue.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality strictly follow the guidelines provided in Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2019-01 dated May 17, 2019 to ensure amended rates of fees or charges are reasonable and just sufficient to recover the direct fixed cost and variable costs of rendering services and also create a local government unit with business-friendly environment.
observations.managementAction:
With regard to Republic Act (RA) Nos. 7160 and 11032 or the “Local Government Code of 1991” and the “Ease of Doing Business and Efficient Government Service Delivery” respectively, our Municipal Ordinance No. 174-S-2019 was compliant with all the provisions of said RA. Unfortunately, we were somehow amiss with regard to the guidelines set forth in the DILG-DOF Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2019-01 dated 17 May 2019.
Now that we are already made aware of the requirements of the said JMC of DILG and DOF, we will ensure that guidelines set therein are fully observed and complied with.
observations.reason: Ordinance compliant with RA 7160 and RA 11032 but oversight regarding DILG-DOF JMC 2019-01 occurred; Municipality commits to fully observe JMC requirements for future fee revisions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the GAD Focal Point Person to submit GAD Plans and Budget to the DILG Regional Office for review and approval; require the focal person to establish a GAD Database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation which will form part of the overall management information system of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor had the GAD Focal Person submit the GAD Plans and Budget to DILG for review and the Municipality has started actions to comply with the GAD requirements. The Municipality has ongoing efforts to establish GAD database and to integrate GAD PPAs.
observations.reason: Copy of the transmittal of GAD Plans and Budget endorsed to DILG was not furnished to the audit team. The GAD Database was not yet established as of December 31, 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Engineer publicize the projects/programs/activities in conspicuous places and in the project venue; require contractors to install safety signages and provide protective gears to laborers; explain/justify non-installation of billboards and signboards and refund of amounts if applicable.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer implemented some projects and conducted inspections. The Municipality had not yet submitted documents showing installation of safety signage, nor the refund documents relative to non-installation of signboards. The municipal engineering office started actions on project implementation and assessment.
observations.reason: Documents showing installation of safety signages and use of protective gears, and justification/ refund documents were not submitted to the audit team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor assist barangay officials to re-establish and operate again their MRFs; that the Municipal Mayor activate the Municipal Agriculturist to assist; that the Municipal Government implement the approved Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan and submit status reports.
observations.managementAction: An Ecological Solid Waste Management Stakeholders Consultative Meeting was conducted on August 02, 2020. Plans for rehabilitation of MRF, reinstitution of Municipal Solid Waste Management Board, reactivation of Barangay SWM Councils and procurement of new dump truck were made. Rehabilitation bidding was initiated August 25, 2020 and a new dump truck was purchased and delivered.
observations.reason: Implementation delayed due to Taal Volcano eruption and COVID-19 pandemic; Municipal MRF rehabilitation and other actions were still in process and documentation of prior implementation was not furnished for verification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality allocate one percent (1%) of its IRA to strengthen and implement programs of the Municipal Council for the Protection of Children (MCPC) and prioritize preparation of Annual Work and Financial Plan, Local Development Plan for Children, Local Investment Plan for Children, Local Code for Children and other required outputs.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality reorganized the MCPC per Administrative Order No. 18 series of 2018. The Municipality stated it had allocated one percent (1%) of IRA by attribution to different offices to strengthen implementation of programs; prepared Annual Work and Financial Plan for Children and included children’s report in the State of the Municipal Address.
observations.reason: Although reorganized, indicators of functionality (regular meetings, separate budget appropriation of 1% IRA specifically for MCPC) were not fully evidenced; allocation had been by attribution to different offices rather than a distinct MCPC appropriation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC in coordination with the Municipal Engineer comply with the semestral submission of the PMR pursuant to Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 03-15.
observations.managementAction: The BAC conducted project monitoring and some PMRs were transmitted; however the PMR transmittal and project monitoring reports duly received by the GPPB were not furnished to the audit team.
observations.reason: PMR documents duly furnished to GPPB were not transmitted to the audit team; the Board lacked the full set of semestral PMRs to formulate accurate procurement regulations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant set-up and maintain Special Accounts in the General Fund for the monitoring of operating performance and viability of the Municipality’s economic enterprises and 20% Development Fund, and to record all transactions pertaining to these enterprises in the corresponding subsidiary ledgers.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality submitted Financial Statements for General Fund, SEF and Trust Fund only. The Accounting Office is to set up separate books and subsidiary ledgers for economic enterprises and the 20% Development Fund.
observations.reason: Separate books and financial statements for economic enterprises and the 20% Development Fund were not provided to the audit team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exhaust efforts to obtain sufficient information on dormant accounts, conduct required analysis and verification including confirmation letters, and if accounts cannot be traced, submit request for write-off supported by Sanggunian resolution and certification of management validation and aging schedule.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accounting Office is in the process of checking and securing information on the dormant accounts and will perform necessary validation and prepare documents for possible write-off if warranted.
observations.reason: The Office has yet to fully identify the debtors and verify the balances; necessary supporting documents and actions for write-off, if applicable, were not yet provided to the audit team.

observations.recommendation:
Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal
Mayor require the following:
a. The General Services Officer conduct regular reconciliation of RPCPPE with the
accounting records to fully identify the discrepancies noted. With regard to
disposal, complete the necessary documents and perform the subsequent
procedures in accordance with the circular. Likewise, ensure that all PPEs are
fully supported with Property Cards and fully updated Acknowledgement Receipt
for Equipment;
b. The Municipal Engineering Office provide technical assistance with the GSO in the
conduct of physical count of infrastructure assets of the Municipality. Have the
results of the count documented and submit to Accounting Office for
reconciliation. Assist the Municipal Accounting Office in determining the useful
life of infrastructure assets and other equipment; and
c. The Accounting Department submit copies of PPE Ledger Cards and Lapsing
Schedule of PPE to the audit team for CY 2020 onwards. Have those records
reconciled with the GSO and document the results to be the bases of adjustments,
if any. Coordinate with the department heads to determine the useful life of those
PPEs and be the basis for the computation of depreciation. Have it documented to
support the adjusting entries to be made to fairly present their net book value.
Lastly, review the breakdown of the items per PPE account and ensure that there
is consistent application of accounting policies in recording PPEs. If there is
reclassification needed, prepare adjusting journal entry and have it supported
with the necessary documentation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the following:
a. Municipal Treasurer continue coordinating with the Municipal Accountant to zero
out the unreconciled balance and present a reliable RPT and SET Receivables in
the financial statements; and
b. Municipal Treasurer continue to devise new strategies to collect all delinquent real
property taxes and encourage the taxpayer to pay their dues on time. Conduct
verification on those outstanding balances to determine the accuracy of the
receivables and its collectability.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget
Officer consider the above mentioned observations/deficiencies in the preparation of
Notes to Financial Statements Reconciliation of Net Cash Flows from Operating
Activities to Surplus/(Deficit) and Notes to Financial Statements Reconciliation of
Budget Actual Amounts to Actual Amounts in the Statement of Financial Performance
and comply with the stated objective of providing additional information to the
intended users of the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to effect the
cancellation of the stale checks and the Municipal Accountant to make proper
adjustments in the municipality’s books of accounts to fairly present its financial
statements as of December 31, 2019.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the municipal
records with the province’s books of accounts to establish the correct balances of
the reciprocal accounts and present fairly the balances of inter-agency receivables
and payables in the financial statements.
Particularly, the Municipal Accountant request from the Provincial Accountant
the copies of the JEVs showing all the transactions recorded in the books of
accounts of the Province that should be taken up in the Municipality’s books of
accounts.

We recommended that the Municipality reconcile employees’ records with the
GSIS, PhilHealth and PAG-IBIG to identify the affected employees on the unremitted
balances and immediately remit the amounts due to these agencies and identify the
cause/s of the over remittance for some accounts and make the proper adjustments
to avoid the incurrence of unnecessary financial expenses in the form of imposed
penalties. Likewise, review the taxes withheld from employees, suppliers and
contractors to ensure that all withheld taxes are remitted in the prescribed period to
avoid penalties and surcharges.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the following:
a. The Municipal Accountant immediately submit the liquidation reports if not yet
submitted and prepare the check for the source agency to refund the unexpended
balance. See to it that future fund transfers are fully implemented within the time
frame to avoid delay in liquidation.
b. The Head of the Municipal Planning and Development Office coordinate with the
concerned department heads to enforce the utilization and implementation of
those projects that are not yet implemented while pursue the continuation of
those on-going utilization. Likewise, see to it that their respective time frames and
schedules are strictly monitored and evaluated to avoid delays in its implementation
c. Coordinate with the concerned officials and departments heads to obtain
information on those long outstanding accounts. Secure the necessary
documents to properly identify the creditor/national government agency to whom
it is due. Once the specific agency unit has been identified, verify the reported
balances in the subsidiary ledgers through confirmation letters and exhaust efforts
to fully ascertain the validity and legality of those obligations

We recommended that the Municipality endeavor for a balanced budget or
incur expenditures within the limit of its appropriations and refrain from the practice
of not recording lawful expenses in the books of accounts of the Municipality in the
period the expenses were incurred and later through sangguniang bayan resolution
to be charged to its current budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly comply with the
previously cited auditing and accounting laws and rules and regulations. Submit the
disbursement voucher and supporting documents to the Auditor within the
prescribed period to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the persons determined as liable to the discrepancy
settle the amount immediately. The persons liable are: Former Municipal Mayor
Gualberto R. Silva for approving the payment and certifying the validity, propriety and
legality of the claim and certifying the acceptance of the project; the Municipal
Engineer, for certifying the completion of the project and AEI Industrial Trade and
Solutions, Co., for accepting the payment.
Please be reminded that the disallowance upon notification not settled within six (6)
months from receipt of notice will become final and executory.

We recommended that the Local School Board strictly comply with the
provisions of Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017 of the Department of Education,
Department of Budget and Management and Department of the Interior and Local
Government in order to ensure that Special Education Fund (SEF) will be utilized for
the intended purpose or according to the identified priority areas listed in the Annual
Implementation Plan (AIP) culled from the DepEd-approved School Improvement
Plan (SIP).

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the efficient and effective
utilization of the 20% Development Fund which is primarily intended to fund projects
that shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and
environmental outcomes.
We further recommended the strict observance of Section 3.2 of Joint Memorandum
Circular No. 1 Series of 2007 dated March 8, 2007 of Department of the Interior and
Local Government, National Economic and Development Authority, Department of
Budget and Management and Department of Finance that the Local Finance
Committee (LFC) and Local Development Council (LDC) ensure that the Annual
Investment Program is the annual slice of the Local Development Investment
Program (LDIP) and adhere to the prescribed format provided in Section 8.6 of Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 1, series of 2016 of DILG, NEDA, DBM and DOF dated
November 18, 2016 in the preparation of Local Development Investment Program
(LDIP).

Based on the forgoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRM)
to enforce the implementation of the programs and projects of the Municipality
under 70% Mitigation Fund, Continuing Appropriation and Trust Liability –
MDRRMF. Have those programs completed within the intended time to avoid delay
in the delivery of services and benefits to the constituents.
b. Require the Municipal Budget Officer to conduct reconciliation with the Municipal
Accountant and the MDRRM Officer to monitor the sources and utilization of 5%
LDRRMF. Document the reconciliation made and submit copies of the result to the
audit team.
c. The Municipal Accountant transfer the unspent LDRRM Fund of 70% MOOE and
30% QRF to its proper account in the Trust Fund books. Document the transfer
made and submit copies of the adjusting entries to the audit team. Likewise,
provide disclosure to the notes to financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly comply with the provisions of
Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of Philippine Commission on Women,
Department of the Interior and Local Government, Department of Budget and
Management and National Economic and Development Authority in the
implementation of the mainstreaming of gender perspectives in its locality as well as
in the creation of GAD Monitoring and Evaluating Team.
We further recommended stringent compliance with the provision of Section
5(2) of the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016 of the
Philippine Commission on Women, Department of the Interior and Local Government,
Department of Budget and Management and National Economic and Development
Authority that the Municipality shall submit her Annual Gender and Development
(GAD) Plan and Budget (GPB) to the Provincial Planning and Development Office
(PPDO) of the Provincial Government to ensure the alignment of the municipal GAD
PPAs with the priorities of the province and then to the DILG Provincial Office for its
review and endorsement to the concerned LGU for incorporation in her annual
budget to be enacted by her Local Sanggunian.

We recommended that the Municipality not only reorganized the Municipal
Council for the Protection of Children (MCPC) but also activate, strengthen, and
sustain the already reorganized MCPC. Allocate the mandatory budget of one (1)
percent of internal revenue allotment and prioritize in the Council’s activities the
preparation of Annual Work and Financial Plan, Local Development Plan for Children,
Local Investment Plan for Children, Local Code for Children, Local State of Children’s
Report, Children’s Profile and the establishment, maintenance and updating of data
base on children for the strengthening and proper implementation of the programs of
the council.

We recommended that the Municipality fully implement the Municipal Solid
Waste Management Plan covering the period CY 2016 to 2026 pursuant to the
provisions of Republic Act No. 9003 or “Philippine Ecological Solid Waste
Management Act of 2000” that espouses on the implementation of the 3R or the
reduce, reuse and recycle concept with focus on reduction of pollution and waste
generation.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly observe the provisions of
Sections 321 and 336 of Republic Act No. 7160 and Article 417 454(b)of the Act
Implementing Rules and Regulations and promote the Code objectives of
discouraging careless preparation of the annual budget or any practice that
undermines the integration of planning and budgeting at the local level.

We recommended that the Municipality particularly the concerned local
government officials strictly observe the provisions of Articles 417 and 418 of the
Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA No. 7160. Local government funds shall
only be available for expenditures pursuant to appropriation ordinance enacted by
the local sanggunian based on the laws, rules and regulations governing its
operations.
We recommended that the GSO maintain the Property Cards and the accounting office prepares corresponding equipment/property ledger cards to serve as their subsidiary records of the PPE accounts.
observations.managementAction: Local Chief Executive to create an Inventory Team conducted the complete physical count of PPE and submit the required RCPI and RPCPPE as required under Section 124 of the manual on NGAS for LGUs. Copy of the RPCPPE had been submitted to the audit team. Also, copies of property cards from the GSO and the accounting Ledger Cards were not submitted for verification.
observations.reason: The Municipality still failed to submit the Report of Physical Count of Inventories and RPCPPE to the current audit team. Likewise, copies of property cards and the accounting Ledger Cards were not submitted for verification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant reconcile their records to present reliable RPT and SET Receivables in the financial statements. Municipal Assessor to timely submit Monthly Report of Assessment to the Municipal Treasurer for reconciliation of their individual records. Municipal Accountant to maintain the necessary Subsidiary Ledgers (SL) for the RPT and SET Receivables in order to validate the balances of the accounts and to ensure reliability of balances reported in the financial statements and to ensure monthly reporting.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality had submitted the Report of Delinquency of RPT and SET Receivable. The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer are on ongoing reconciliation of their RPT/SET records. The Municipal Assessor had submitted the Monthly Report of Assessment for CY 2019. The Municipal Accountant had submitted the Subsidiary Ledgers (SL) for CY 2019; however the subsidiary ledgers of RPT and SET receivable were not included in the submitted SL to verify the reported balance in the Financial Statements with the source documents.
observations.reason: The discrepancy has decreased compared to CY 2018 but the unreconciled balances remain. Copies of documents substantiating reconciliation were not furnished to the audit team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MDRRMO enforce implementation of programs/projects under Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (LDRRMF) and prepare and submit the Monthly Report of Sources and Utilization of DRRMF in the format required by COA Circular No. 2012-002; that the Municipal Accountant transfer unspent balances to Trust Fund as required; and that Projects funded and completed be made available for disaster risk reduction and management activities.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Social Welfare and Development had submitted the LDRRMFIP for CY 2019. The Municipality submitted some Monthly Reports on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF which were received by the audit team in August and October 2019. The Municipality had submitted Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF for CY 2019. The MDRRMO had submitted part of the required documents. Some balances from continuing appropriations were considered as savings and added in the Supplemental Budget No. 1. The Municipality had submitted the AIP for the 5% LDRRM fund but there were differences between AIP and Budget Ordinance.
observations.reason: Some reports were submitted late; PPAs funded and completed from prior years were not clearly included in the LDRRMFIP; some unexpended balances for CY 2019 were not transferred to Trust Fund books as required and notes to financial statements lacked full disclosure.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the 20% Development Fund is fully utilized so as to realize its intended purpose of achieving desirable socio-economic and environmental development; that the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and members of the Local Development Council submit in writing explanation and justification on reasons why they failed to coordinate, monitor and evaluate the implementation of the Municipality’s development programs and projects.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government had partially implemented various PPAs. The Municipal Planning and Development Office submitted the Annual Investment Program and Supplemental Investment Program. Some PPAs were implemented and some were not due to various issues such as late bidding, Taal eruption, and lack of manpower.
observations.reason: The Municipality implemented only portions of the AIP; several PPAs remain unimplemented at yearend and justification/validation documents on reasons for non-implementation were not provided to the audit team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the specific focal person, the BAC members, and the Sanggunian Members to immediately implement the required activities for the Supplementary Feeding Program based on the set timeline to improve and sustain the nutritional status of the target beneficiaries. Also expedite resolution for purchase of goods and ensure liquidation and return of excess funds.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality implemented the Supplementary Feeding Program and liquidated the fund transfer. A check was issued in March 2019 to return the excess fund to the source agency.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Engineer ensure that infrastructure projects undergo detailed engineering investigations, surveys and designs and that the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and Municipal Engineer properly plan the ensuing construction work and utilize the completed multipurpose gymnasium; also submit explanations/justifications.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government conducted project inspections. The BAC/TAS had findings on construction phases; some project documents for evaluation were not submitted. The Municipality attempted to address issues and will utilize the gymnasium.
observations.reason: Some evaluation documents and justifications on deficiencies in construction were not provided to the audit team; installation and utilization plans and supporting documents were not furnished.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit liquidation reports if not yet submitted and prepare checks for source agencies to refund unexpended balances; the Municipal Planning and Development Office coordinate to implement projects; coordinate with concerned officials to obtain information on long outstanding accounts and verify balances through confirmation letters.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office to submit liquidation reports and prepare checks for refund once validated. Coordination with stakeholders to implement remaining projects within timeframe was ongoing.
observations.reason: Some fund transfers for completed projects remained outstanding; documents identifying specific creditor agencies and supporting liquidation documents were not fully provided; some payables aged beyond two years remained.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct department heads/personnel concerned to comply with completion and submission to the Audit Team of the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation (AAPSI) for Part III of CY 2018 AAR and on every AAR thereafter.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality had submitted the AAPSI to the audit team for CY 2018 and Prior Years’ Audit Findings in August 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer evaluate the validity of the long outstanding liabilities and seek authority from the Sanggunian for reversion of the valid balances to Unappropriated Surplus per RA No. 3526 and EO No. 109 s.1993 and remit amounts due to national government agencies.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office is checking and evaluating the long outstanding liabilities and is in the process of gathering documents relative to those balances. Coordination with the Sanggunian will be made for appropriate actions.
observations.reason: The Municipality was in the process of ascertaining balances and obtaining supporting documents; some long outstanding payables remained and were yet to be validated and reverted or remitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the GSO re-claim the unit motorcycle turned over to the Police Provincial Office, document acceptance of donated assets by deed of donation and acceptance, and that donated assets be recorded properly in the accounting records showing acquisition cost and net book value; dispose unserviceable vehicles as required.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality conducted inspections and recorded the donated ambulance in the General Journal though the net book value was reflected in the Journal Entry Voucher. The GSO and Accounting Office coordinated on disposition and documentation; however some supporting documents and proper recording details were not fully submitted.
observations.reason: Supporting documents showing proper recording of donated assets, deeds of donation, and disposal documents for unserviceable vehicles were not fully furnished; net book value recording did not show acquisition cost as required.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality reconcile employees’ records with GSIS, PhilHealth and PAG-IBIG to identify affected employees on unremitted balances and immediately remit the amounts due; identify causes of over-remittance and make proper adjustments; and review taxes withheld from employees, suppliers and contractors to ensure timely remittance to avoid penalties.
observations.managementAction: Reconciliation is ongoing for employees’ records with GSIS, PhilHealth and PAG-IBIG to identify affected employees and to remit amounts due. The Municipal Accounting Office will exert effort to reconcile and identify causes of discrepancies and review withholding taxes.
observations.reason: Reconciliations were ongoing and not yet completed; discrepancies between withheld amounts and remittances remained at year-end and supporting subsidiary ledgers were not fully maintained or submitted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Local Chief Executive to create an Inventory Team to
conduct complete physical count of Inventories and PPE and submit the required
RCPI and RPCPPE as required under Section 124 of manual on NGAS for LGUs.
We also recommended that the GSO maintain the Property Cards and the
accounting office prepares corresponding equipment/property ledger cards to
serve as their subsidiary records of the Inventories and PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the following:
 Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records to
present reliable RPT and SET Receivables in the financial statements.
 Municipal Assessor to timely submit Monthly Report of Assessment of Real
Properties and Individual Tax Payers Assessment to the Municipal Treasurer for
reconciliation of their individual records.
 Municipal Accountant to maintain the necessary subsidiary ledgers for the RPT
and SET Receivables in order to validate the balances of the accounts presented
in the financial statements and to ensure reliability of the balances reported
monthly.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the official concern to fully
utilize the fund for the implementation of programs/projects under Local Disaster
Risk Reduction Management Fund(LDRRMF) for the benefit of constituents
particularly in the affected and vulnerable areas and to refer to Section 5.1.10 of
COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and Section 21 of Republic
Act 10121 for the recording and reporting of LDRRMF and use of 30 percent and
70 percent calamity fund.
We recommended that the MDRRMO prepare and submit the Monthly Report of
Sources and Utilization of DRRMF duly certified correct by the Accountant on or
before the 15th day after the end of each month in accordance with the format
provided by COA Circular No. 2012-002.
We recommended that the balances of all projects funded and already completed
formed part of savings be made available for use in the disaster risk reduction and
35
management activities as provided in the LDRRMFIP as provided by Item 5.1.11 of
the circular mentioned.
We also recommended that the MDRRMO prepare and submit the LDRRMF
Investment Plan in accordance with the format provided by the same circular to
reflect the allocation for disaster mitigation, prevention and preparedness with
details as to projects and activities to be funded, as well as the list of projects and
activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the GAD Focal Point Person to submit GAD Plans and Budget to
the DILG Regional Office for review and approval before its integration
to the municipality’s annual appropriation;
b. require the focal point person to spearhead the establishment of a GAD
database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning,
programming and policy formulation which will form part of the overall
management information system of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the 20% Development
Fund is fully utilized so as to realize its intended purpose of achieving desirable
socio-economic development and environmental outcome.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Planning and Development Officer and the members of the Local Development
Council to submit in writing an explanation and justification on the reasons why
the Council failed to coordinate, monitor, and evaluate the implementation of the
Municipality’s development programs and projects.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor direct the focal person, the BAC
members, and the legislative body or Sanggunian Members to implement
immediately the required activities on Supplementary Feeding Program based on
the set timeline in order to improve and sustain the nutritional status of the target
